findings.title: Package has no consumer -- mcp-hub is still orphan code
findings.code_snippet:
export { McpHub } from './mcp-hub'
export type { ConnectEnabledResult, ServerStatus } from './mcp-hub'
export { McpClientManager } from './mcp-client-manager'
export { McpServerStore } from './server-store'
export { createTransport } from './transport-factory'
findings.description: No package in the monorepo depends on @proj-airi/mcp-hub. A grep for '@proj-airi/mcp-hub' across all package.json files returns only the package's own package.json. The exports are not imported by any app (stage-tamagotchi, stage-web, server) or other package. This was flagged in iter1 as review-code-002 and remains unresolved. Per CLAUDE.md orphan detection rules, code without a live entry point is dead-on-arrival.
findings.suggestion: Wire mcp-hub into at least one consumer before merging. For example: (1) Add @proj-airi/mcp-hub as a dependency of apps/stage-tamagotchi or apps/server. (2) Import McpHub in the Anima orchestrator or a new IPC handler. (3) Provide a minimal end-to-end path: UI config -> IPC -> McpHub.addServer() -> McpHub.connectServer() -> tools available to LLM. Even a single thin integration proves the package is reachable.

findings.title: aggregateTools collision handling is asymmetric -- first tool keeps short name
findings.description: When two servers expose a tool with the same name (e.g., 'search'), the first server's tool is stored under the plain name 'search', while the second server's tool is stored under 'serverB__search'. The first server's tool is never retroactively namespaced. This creates an asymmetric API: callers see 'search' (from server A) and 'serverB__search' (from server B) but have no way to discover which server owns the un-namespaced 'search'. With 3+ servers sharing a name, only the first keeps the short key. This is a correctness issue: the behavior depends on insertion order into the clients Map and is non-obvious to consumers.
findings.suggestion: When a collision is detected, retroactively namespace the FIRST tool as well. Implementation: (1) Track tool origins: Map<toolName, serverId>. (2) On collision, rename the existing entry from 'toolName' to 'originalServerId__toolName', then add 'newServerId__toolName'. This ensures all colliding tools are namespaced symmetrically. Alternatively, always namespace all tools (simpler, more predictable).

findings.title: No structured logging anywhere in the package
findings.description: The entire mcp-hub package has zero logging. Server connections, disconnections, failures, tool aggregation, config changes -- none are logged. This was flagged in iter1 (review-code-007) and remains unresolved. Per CLAUDE.md, structured logging with @guiiai/logg is the standard pattern across this monorepo. MCP server lifecycle events are operational data that will be needed for production debugging. The silent catch blocks in removeServer (line 41) and transport cleanup (mcp-client-manager.ts line 28) make this especially important -- errors are being swallowed with no observability.
findings.suggestion: Inject @guiiai/logg via useLogg() pattern. Log at INFO level: server added/removed/updated, connection established/closed, tool aggregation results. Log at WARN level: connection failures (in connectEnabled failed array), tool name collisions. Log at ERROR level: store corruption, transport creation failures. Include serverId, serverName, and duration_ms in structured fields. Replace the /* best-effort cleanup */ and /* proceed with removal */ comments with actual logger.warn() calls.

findings.title: disconnectAll silently discards failure details from Promise.allSettled
findings.description: Promise.allSettled is the right choice here (good fix from iter1), but the rejection reasons are filtered for and then completely discarded. The failures array is checked for length but the actual error messages are never logged, returned, or propagated. This means if 3 out of 5 servers fail to disconnect, the caller (McpHub.shutdown) has no idea which servers failed or why. Combined with the absence of logging (review-code-003), these errors vanish entirely. Additionally, clients.clear() is only called on failure, leaving stale entries for servers whose disconnect() threw -- this is the correct intent (clean up unreachable clients) but the reasoning should be documented.
findings.suggestion: At minimum, collect and log the failure reasons. When logging is added: `for (const f of failures) { logger.warn('Failed to disconnect', { reason: f.reason }) }`. Consider returning the failures or throwing an AggregateError if any disconnect failed, so shutdown() can report it. Add a comment explaining why clients.clear() is called on failure (to release references to clients that could not be gracefully closed).

findings.title: SSE/HTTP URL validation only checks non-empty, not valid URL format
findings.description: For SSE and HTTP transports, the URL is only validated for truthiness (non-empty). Any non-empty string passes validation, including 'not-a-url', 'javascript:alert(1)', or relative paths like '../../../etc/passwd'. While this won't cause command injection (unlike stdio), it means invalid URLs are persisted to SQLite and the error only surfaces later when createMCPClient tries to connect -- producing an obscure network error instead of a clear validation error at add/update time. The command allowlist for stdio was well-implemented; URL validation should match that level of rigor.
findings.suggestion: Add URL format validation: `try { new URL(url) } catch { throw new Error('Invalid URL for ${transport} transport: ${url}') }`. Optionally restrict to http/https protocols: `if (!['http:', 'https:'].includes(new URL(url).protocol)) throw new Error(...)`. This catches malformed URLs at the boundary (add/update) rather than at connection time.

findings.title: removeServer swallows disconnect error without logging or reporting
findings.description: When removing a server, if the disconnect fails, the error is completely swallowed. The comment 'proceed with removal even if disconnect fails' documents the intent, which is reasonable -- you do want to remove the config even if the child process is stuck. However, the disconnect failure could indicate a zombie process (stdio transport) that will leak resources. Without logging, there is no way to detect this in production. This is especially concerning because stdio transports spawn child processes that may not terminate cleanly.
findings.suggestion: Once logging is added (see review-code-003), replace the empty catch with: `catch (err) { logger.warn('Failed to disconnect server before removal', { serverId: id, error: err }) }`. The behavior (proceed with removal) should remain the same, but the failure should be observable.

findings.title: Transport factory returns heterogeneous union with plain objects for SSE/HTTP
findings.description: The McpTransport type is a union of a class instance (StdioMCPTransport) and two plain object literals for SSE and HTTP. The plain objects lack the lifecycle methods (start/close) that the StdioMCPTransport class has. In mcp-client-manager.ts line 21, the transport is cast as `transport as McpTransport` when passed to createMCPClient, suggesting a type mismatch. This was noted in iter1 (review-code-009). If @ai-sdk/mcp's createMCPClient accepts these plain objects for SSE/HTTP, a brief comment documenting this would prevent future confusion. If it does not, SSE/HTTP connections will fail at runtime with a confusing error.
findings.suggestion: Verify whether @ai-sdk/mcp's createMCPClient accepts plain {type, url, headers} objects for SSE/HTTP transports by checking the SDK documentation or source. If confirmed, add a comment: `// @ai-sdk/mcp accepts plain config objects for SSE/HTTP transports`. If not confirmed, use the appropriate SDK transport classes. The cast on mcp-client-manager.ts line 21 should be removed once the types align properly.

positive_observations: Excellent iter1 fix: Command injection vulnerability resolved with ALLOWED_COMMANDS allowlist and SHELL_METACHAR_PATTERN regex in server-store.ts -- well-implemented security boundary with tests proving both allow and reject paths
Excellent iter1 fix: connectEnabled() now uses Promise.allSettled with ConnectEnabledResult return type, properly handling partial failures and reporting which servers succeeded vs failed
Excellent iter1 fix: update() now calls validateConfig(merged) after merge, closing the validation bypass gap
Excellent iter1 fix: safeJsonParse() wraps JSON.parse with try-catch and context-rich error message, protecting against corrupt database rows
Excellent iter1 fix: aggregateTools() now detects tool name collisions and namespaces the second occurrence (though asymmetrically -- see finding review-code-002)
Server config persisted in SQLite with WAL mode -- correct 12-factor stateless pattern
All tests use real SQLite (temp files) and real stdio MCP server fixture (echo-mcp-server.ts) -- no mocks, no InMemoryRepository, no jest.fn(). Strong adherence to testing rules
Clean SRP separation: types.ts, server-store.ts, transport-factory.ts, mcp-client-manager.ts, mcp-hub.ts each have a single clear responsibility
Parameterized queries throughout server-store.ts with zero SQL string concatenation
Good persistence tests proving SQLite round-trip survives close/reopen, including the McpHub end-to-end persistence test
